front: Building: Johnson's personal residence in New Canaan, Connecticut; he lived and died there with partner David Whitney
Building: modeled on Mies van der Rohe's Farnsworth House
Building: brick cylinder conceals the bathroom — the only fully enclosed space
Building: 'Da Monsta' — red-and-black visitor's pavilion on the grounds
Building: Painting Gallery — three circular rooms inspired by Agamemnon's tomb, with rotating poster rack to view 42 artworks two at a time
Building: grounds landscaped to resemble Nicolas Poussin's The Burial of Phocion
Building: Monument to Lincoln Kirstein — secret inscription atop a 'staircase to nowhere'
Building: cylindrical Donald Judd sculpture stands near the house
Building: collaboration with Mies van der Rohe; located at 375 Park Avenue, New York
Building: Johnson designed the Four Seasons Restaurant inside
Building: Johnson insisted the blinds could only be set to three positions
Building: scored 3 out of 100 for Energy Star rating
Building: John Logan's play Red depicts Rothko calling Philip Johnson after dining badly at the Four Seasons Restaurant inside this building
Building: open pediment on top earned it the nickname 'Chippendale Building'; located at 550 Madison Avenue
Building: Johnson's defection from the Modernist 'Whites' to the Postmodernist 'Grays' was symbolized by this building's ornamental Chippendale top
Building: PPG Place in Pittsburgh — features 231 glass spires
Building: Gates of Europe in Madrid — two towers that mirror each other, each inclined at 15 degrees
Building: Crystal Cathedral in Garden Grove, California — emphasizes natural light; home of The Hour of Power broadcast
Building: Kneses Tifereth Israel Synagogue in Port Chester, NY — designed for free to atone for pro-Nazi sympathies
Building: JFK Memorial in Dallas — criticized by one critic as 'mammoth Lego blocks'
Building: Chapel of Thanksgiving in Dallas's Thanks-Giving Square (1976) — inspired by the Malwiya minaret in Samarra
Building: designed the reflecting pool in front of the Rothko Chapel in Houston, which contains Barnett Newman's Broken Obelisk
Building: IDS Tower in Minneapolis
Building: Amon Carter Museum in Fort Worth, Texas
Building: New York State Pavilion at the 1964 World's Fair
Architect: won the first Pritzker Architecture Prize in 1979
Architect: had pro-Nazi sympathies in the 1930s; atoned by designing Kneses Tifereth Israel Synagogue for free
Architect: defected from the Modernist 'Whites' (led by Peter Eisenman and Richard Meier) to the Postmodernist 'Grays' — the highest-profile such defection in American architecture
Architect: Robert Venturi's Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture praised his Wiley House but criticized the Glass House as 'bland architecture'
Architect: coined the term 'International Style' in a book co-written with Henry-Russell Hitchcock, published concurrently with a MoMA exhibition
